target_output.title: Telco Edge Cloud Orchestration Platform
target_output.executive_summary: A highly resilient, massive-scale infrastructure platform designed to manage edge computing workloads across thousands of physical 5G cell towers. By deploying K3s (a lightweight Kubernetes distribution) onto bare-metal x86 servers, the platform brings computational power directly to the network edge. To resolve critical deployment timeouts caused by unstable cellular backhaul connections, the architecture heavily leverages a pull-based GitOps methodology via FluxCD, ensuring autonomous, reliable software reconciliation that guarantees eventual consistency across 10,000+ physically isolated locations.
target_output.architecture_overview: The architecture eliminates centralized deployment bottlenecks in favor of edge autonomy. Bare metal servers at the cell towers are initially bootstrapped with a minimal Ubuntu OS and K3s via Ansible over SSH. Once K3s is active, a FluxCD controller is installed inside the cluster. To satisfy the constraint regarding intermittent connectivity, the architecture strictly forbids inbound 'push' deployments. Instead, the FluxCD controller periodically initiates outbound HTTPS requests to a centralized, highly available corporate GitLab repository. It polls for changes to declarative Kubernetes manifests. If a cell tower's network connection drops, the local K3s cluster continues running its existing workloads uninterrupted. When the connection restores, Flux pulls the latest Git commit and locally applies the state reconciliation. This distributed, zero-trust approach ensures that the central management network is never responsible for maintaining concurrent TCP connections to 10,000 distinct edge nodes.
target_output.technology_stack: Kubernetes Distribution: K3s. GitOps Operator: FluxCD. OS Provisioning: Ansible, PXE Boot. Operating System: Ubuntu Server 22.04. Configuration Management: Kustomize, Helm. Source Control: GitLab Enterprise. Infrastructure: Bare Metal x86 (Cell Site Cabinets). Observability: Prometheus Edge, Thanos.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Pull-Based Fleet Management allowing tens of thousands of clusters to update autonomously without relying on fragile, centralized deployment pipelines. Immutable Infrastructure Definitions forcing all edge configuration changes to be peer-reviewed and committed to Git prior to execution. Disconnected Operation Survivability guaranteeing that critical edge routing software continues functioning even if the tower loses its fiber backhaul connection to the core network. Inbound Port Lockdown physically securing edge nodes by requiring all management software to operate exclusively via outbound polling.
target_output.system_components: Central GitLab Repository acting as the single source of truth for all cluster states. K3s Control Plane executing locally on the bare-metal edge hardware. FluxCD Source Controller fetching raw Git artifacts. FluxCD Kustomize Controller applying the downloaded manifests to the local API server. Ansible Bastion Host handling initial zero-day bootstrapping.
target_output.workflows: Initial Provisioning: Technician mounts the server in the cell tower cabinet and connects power. Ansible connects via SSH, installs Docker, downloads the K3s binary, and installs FluxCD, pointing it to the `edge-fleet` GitLab repo. Automated Application Update: Platform engineer merges a Pull Request updating the Nginx image tag from `v1.2` to `v1.3` in the central repository. The FluxCD controller on Tower A polls GitLab and detects the new commit. Tower A pulls the manifest and updates its local pod. Tower B's network is currently down. Two hours later, Tower B reconnects, polls GitLab, detects the commit, and updates its local pod, achieving eventual consistency without any human intervention or failed pipeline alerts.
target_output.data_model: No relational data is stored. System state is defined entirely as YAML text files within the Git repository. Flux organizes these definitions using Kustomize overlays: `base/` containing the core Nginx deployment, and `overlays/tower-102/` containing node-specific overrides (like static IP allocations or regional configuration maps).
target_output.api_design: No external REST APIs are exposed by the infrastructure layer. Internal interaction occurs via the Kubernetes API server running on `localhost:6443` at each edge site, accessed locally by FluxCD or remotely via mTLS secured `kubectl` connections through a secure bastion host during emergency debugging.
target_output.security_and_compliance: The edge nodes exist in physically vulnerable locations (fenced cell towers). The architecture assumes a hostile physical environment. Full Disk Encryption (LUKS) protects the host OS. The network perimeter enforces a zero-trust model; firewall rules drop all inbound traffic to management ports. The edge node authenticates with the central GitLab server using strict, read-only deploy keys rotated automatically. Internal workloads communicate using mTLS certificates generated and rotated by the local K3s control plane.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Infrastructure deployment relies entirely on GitOps. To manage a fleet of 10,000 nodes safely, deployments utilize canary branches and staggered rollouts. A change is merged to the `canary` branch, which only 50 test towers follow. After 24 hours of automated health checks, the change is merged to `production`, triggering the remaining 9,950 towers to pull the update.
target_output.observability: A lightweight Prometheus instance runs on every edge node, scraping hardware metrics (temperature, disk I/O) and K3s metrics. Instead of streaming massive volumes of logs to a central server (which would saturate the backhaul), Prometheus heavily aggregates the data locally and uses Thanos to expose a query API to the central Network Operations Center, transferring data only when explicitly queried.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Hardware costs are massive CapEx investments per cell tower. The architectural choice to use K3s over standard Kubernetes drastically reduces the RAM and CPU overhead required for the control plane, freeing up resources for the actual virtualized telecommunications workloads. The pull-based FluxCD model scales infinitely; the central GitLab server simply serves static Git fetches, easily supporting 100,000+ polling nodes backed by standard CDN caching.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (MVP): Ansible provisioning and K3s deployment on 50 test towers. Phase 2: Integration of FluxCD and migration of standard networking tools to GitOps management. Phase 3: Thanos observability integration for remote metric aggregation. Phase 4: Full rollout to 10,000 towers and onboarding of the Virtualized Radio Access Network (vRAN) container workloads.
target_output.development_guidelines: Engineers must never SSH into an edge node to execute `kubectl apply`; all state mutations must be committed to Git. Helm charts utilized within the edge deployments must bundle their container images into local registries or rely on high-availability corporate proxies, avoiding reliance on public internet registries (like Docker Hub) that could rate-limit 10,000 simultaneous image pulls.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: A malformed Kubernetes manifest is committed to the Git repository, causing all 10,000 edge nodes to pull a breaking change that corrupts their routing configuration. Mitigation: FluxCD is configured with strict health checks and automatic rollback capabilities; if a newly applied manifest causes pod crash loops, Flux automatically reverts the cluster to the last known healthy Git commit. Tradeoff: Adopting a decentralized, pull-based GitOps model severely limits real-time, synchronous feedback during deployments; engineers must wait and query observability dashboards to confirm if a rollout succeeded, rather than watching a synchronous Jenkins pipeline turn green.

ISS-104.description: Memory exhaustion and SLA failure. The Airflow DAG executes Python Xarray on a single massive EC2 node. Processing the 100GB NetCDF file takes 14 hours and crashes due to swap thrashing.

Transitioned output from monolithic NetCDF to chunked Zarr, resolving bottlenecks and dropping execution time to 45 minutes.

target_output.thinking_summary: Revising the agricultural data pipeline to fix single-node OOM crashes (ISS-104). The fix distributes the Xarray workload using Dask on an AWS EMR cluster. Output format is changed to Zarr for parallel S3 writes. Constraints ensure all text sections meet word limits.

fix_report.action_taken: Migrated processing from a monolithic EC2 instance to a distributed AWS EMR cluster. Integrated Dask with Xarray to partition the 100GB NetCDF file across 20 worker nodes. Replaced the monolithic output format with the cloud-optimized Zarr format to enable highly parallelized S3 writes.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Eliminates single-node memory exhaustion. The highly parallelized architecture reduces the processing time of a 100GB dataset from 14 hours to approximately 45 minutes, comfortably satisfying the 2-hour SLA.

target_output.title: Distributed Satellite Imagery Pipeline
target_output.executive_summary: A highly scalable, automated data pipeline engineered to process massive daily satellite datasets for agricultural trading signals. Orchestrated by Apache Airflow and deployed on AWS EMR, the system leverages Python, Xarray, and Dask to distribute complex multi-dimensional array mathematics across clustered compute nodes. By transitioning to cloud-optimized Zarr formats on Amazon S3, the architecture resolves critical out-of-memory bottlenecks, ensuring quantitative analysts receive lightning-fast, highly granular vegetation indices well before the strict market open deadlines.
target_output.architecture_overview: The pipeline operates within an isolated AWS VPC. Apache Airflow schedules a daily DAG that provisions a transient AWS EMR cluster. Instead of downloading complete 100GB NetCDF files, Dask workers stream specific byte-ranges directly from Amazon S3. The workers perform spatial re-gridding and temporal averaging in parallel, ensuring no single node exhausts its RAM footprint. To resolve previous write bottlenecks, the workers push the finalized multidimensional arrays back to S3 concurrently utilizing the Zarr format, which partitions the data into thousands of optimized chunks. Upon completion, the EMR cluster terminates instantly.
target_output.technology_stack: Orchestration relies on Apache Airflow. Distributed computing leverages Python 3.11, Dask, and Xarray. Storage relies strictly on Amazon S3 utilizing NetCDF4 and Zarr formats. Infrastructure is deployed automatically using Terraform on AWS EMR, leveraging EC2 Spot instances to optimize processing costs significantly for the daily execution windows.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Distributed array evaluation executes multi-dimensional mathematical transformations across the cluster without causing massive localized RAM spikes. Spatial re-gridding interpolates satellite pixel resolutions to match standard trading boundaries. Cloud-optimized Zarr export partitions the final arrays into compressed S3 objects for blazing-fast slice queries by internal data scientists analyzing historical trends.
target_output.system_components: Apache Airflow scheduler triggering the daily workflow execution. AWS EMR master node coordinating the dynamic Dask scheduler. Multiple EMR core nodes executing the isolated Python worker processes. S3 ingest buckets holding raw NOAA binaries securely, and S3 analytics buckets serving the finalized trading signals to the research teams.
target_output.workflows: At 04:00 AM, Airflow triggers the DAG, launching the EMR cluster. Dask workers stream the Red and Near-Infrared bands directly from S3 using optimized byte-range requests. The workers calculate the Normalized Difference Vegetation Index across billions of pixels entirely in memory. The final aggregated data is saved concurrently as Zarr files, and the cluster terminates within 45 minutes.
target_output.data_model: Input consists of NetCDF files containing dense 4D arrays mapping time, altitude, latitude, and longitude. Output utilizes Zarr stores, representing multidimensional arrays as partitioned key-value objects stored natively on Amazon S3. The pipeline maintains no relational databases, processing strictly immutable scientific arrays designed for rapid column-based analytical reads.
target_output.api_design: No synchronous REST APIs are hosted by this backend infrastructure. Operations trigger strictly via automated cron schedules. Downstream data scientists interface with the finalized metrics by writing Python scripts in Jupyter Notebooks that seamlessly map the S3 Zarr chunks back into Xarray objects for transparent manipulation.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Meteorological data contains zero PII, minimizing regulatory compliance overhead. The EMR cluster runs in isolated private subnets. S3 buckets enforce strict IAM policies, denying all access except from the specific IAM role assigned to the EMR worker nodes via VPC gateway endpoints, keeping all proprietary data off the public internet.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Infrastructure is codified completely using Terraform modules. Python processing scripts are stored in S3 and pulled onto EMR during the bootstrap phase. The DevOps team maintains a custom Amazon Machine Image for EMR to pre-install specialized scientific Python packages, drastically reducing cluster startup times and preventing pip dependency resolution failures during the daily run.
target_output.observability: The dynamic Dask Dashboard is securely tunneled to visualize the task execution graph and worker memory pressure. AWS CloudWatch captures standard EMR cluster metrics regarding CPU and disk I/O. Airflow triggers immediate Slack alerts upon DAG task failures, ensuring operators can intervene before the 8:00 AM market open.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: EC2 Spot Instances reduce EMR compute costs by roughly sixty percent. The pipeline scales linearly by adjusting the Airflow configuration variables to request additional worker nodes if the daily ingest file size suddenly doubles, preventing SLA breaches.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase one implements a local Python script to validate Xarray math on a subset. Phase two integrates Dask for local parallelization. Phase three deploys the AWS EMR cluster to handle full files. Phase four transitions the final output format to Zarr to permanently fix the S3 write bottlenecks.
target_output.development_guidelines: Define explicit chunk sizes when loading NetCDF files in Xarray to prevent Dask from loading entire arrays into a single worker's memory, causing instant crashes. Avoid triggering `.compute()` mid-pipeline; strictly maintain the lazy-evaluated Dask graph until the final `to_zarr()` export command.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk includes upstream satellite providers delaying data publication, causing pipeline timeouts. Mitigation involves Airflow sensors with timeouts that gracefully fallback to prior day models. Tradeoff accepts slightly degraded predictive accuracy to guarantee trading report delivery before the market bell rings, prioritizing absolute reliability over waiting for perfect telemetry.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Resolved single-node memory exhaustion by officially adopting Dask on AWS EMR. Confirmed Zarr over NetCDF for highly parallelized S3 write performance, accepting the requirement that downstream quants must refactor their loading scripts.

project_goal.value: Automate BGP routing failovers for transoceanic submarine cable cuts

capabilities.rationale: Automated router configuration and telemetry processing

complexity_level.rationale: Global BGP convergence and optical DWDM telemetry

risk_level.rationale: Routing loops cause global internet outages

constraints.value: BGP route withdrawal must execute within 2 seconds of optical signal loss

reasoner_reviews.product: When an anchor cuts a transatlantic cable, terabits of data instantly drop. The automation must reroute via alternative cables immediately.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Using Python and Netmiko over SSH is far too slow to hit the 2-second constraint. We must rely on Bi-directional Forwarding Detection (BFD) directly on the router hardware for millisecond fault detection, using Python only for the post-failover capacity rebalancing.
reasoner_reviews.security: Access to the core routers must be strictly routed through an isolated Out-of-Band (OOB) management network.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: To avoid blackholing traffic, the BGP timers must be aggressively tuned. Python will continuously pull DWDM optical telemetry via gRPC dial-out to InfluxDB to warn operators before the physical cut occurs.

devops.routing: Juniper/Cisco BGP configurations with BFD enabled for sub-second link down detection
devops.automation: Python scripts querying NetBox to rebuild network intent and push config changes via NETCONF

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing an infrastructure project for Submarine Cable Management. Goal is automated BGP failover. Constraint is <2s route withdrawal. The architect noted Python/SSH is too slow, so BFD (Bi-directional Forwarding Detection) is required at the hardware layer, with Python handling telemetry and secondary rebalancing via NETCONF. Output strictly valid JSONL. Quality set to 'strong'.
target_output.title: Submarine Cable Resiliency Infrastructure
target_output.executive_summary: A mission-critical network automation platform designed to protect global internet backbones from catastrophic transoceanic submarine cable cuts. Targeted at Network Operations Center (NOC) engineers, the system combines hardware-level fault detection with intelligent software automation. To satisfy the absolute requirement of sub-2-second traffic rerouting and prevent massive data blackholing, the architecture offloads immediate BGP route withdrawals to hardware-accelerated Bi-directional Forwarding Detection (BFD). Concurrently, a Python-based telemetry engine processes optical signals in real-time, automating secondary capacity rebalancing across surviving links.
target_output.architecture_overview: The architecture operates entirely on-premise within isolated Out-of-Band (OOB) management networks. At the hardware layer, core carrier-grade routers (e.g., Cisco, Juniper) are configured with BFD to monitor physical link states in milliseconds, automatically withdrawing BGP routes the moment a subsea cable is severed. The software automation layer, written in Python, interfaces with these routers via NETCONF and gRPC. Dense Wavelength Division Multiplexing (DWDM) optical equipment streams real-time telemetry (receive/transmit optical power) via gRPC dial-out directly into an InfluxDB time-series database. When BFD handles the immediate failover, the Python automation engine queries the NetBox Source of Truth database, calculates the new optimal traffic distribution, and pushes updated BGP weights via NETCONF to surviving transoceanic links, preventing secondary congestion failures.
target_output.technology_stack: Hardware Protocols: BGP, BFD, DWDM. Automation Runtime: Python 3.11, Netmiko, ncclient (NETCONF). Source of Truth: NetBox. Telemetry Database: InfluxDB 2.0. Telemetry Transport: gRPC Dial-Out, SNMPv3. Visualization: Grafana. Infrastructure: On-Premise Management VMs. Security: Hardware MFA, SSH Keys.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Hardware-Accelerated Failover utilizing BFD to detect severed subsea fibers and withdraw BGP routes in under 50 milliseconds, bypassing slow software polling. Optical Telemetry Streaming ingesting high-frequency light levels from DWDM transponders into InfluxDB to visualize physical degradation before a total snap occurs. Declarative Network Intent relying on NetBox as the absolute source of truth to programmatically recalculate and deploy secondary BGP routing policies. Automated Capacity Rebalancing shifting non-critical traffic to higher-latency terrestrial routes when a primary transatlantic cable is lost.
target_output.system_components: Core Provider Routers executing BGP and BFD in hardware ASIC chips. DWDM Optical Transponders terminating the physical submarine glass. NetBox Database holding intended IP allocations and ASN configurations. Python Automation Workers executing configuration drift remediation. InfluxDB storing continuous optical power metrics. Grafana displaying the global topology state.
target_output.workflows: Cable Cut Scenario: A ship anchor severs a transatlantic fiber. The DWDM transponder loses light. The router's BFD protocol misses three 15ms control packets. The router immediately brings down the BGP peering session, withdrawing the routes and shifting traffic to the backup cable within 50ms. Telemetry & Rebalancing: The Python engine detects the BGP down event via an SNMP trap. It queries InfluxDB, confirming total optical signal loss. It queries NetBox for the surviving network topology. Recognizing the backup cable is nearing capacity, the Python script uses NETCONF to connect to regional edge routers, injecting new BGP communities that reprioritize low-priority bulk traffic to alternative, higher-latency Pacific routes, stabilizing the global backbone.
target_output.data_model: NetBox (PostgreSQL) models the declarative state: Sites, Racks, Devices, Interfaces, IP Addresses, and BGP Sessions. InfluxDB models the dynamic state: `dwdm_rx_power` tagged by `hostname` and `interface_id`. The automation scripts treat NetBox as the immutable schema, pushing configurations that align the physical routers with the database intent.
target_output.api_design: No consumer-facing APIs. Internal automation relies strictly on standard network engineering interfaces: NETCONF (XML over SSH) for configuration mutations, gRPC for high-speed streaming telemetry, and the NetBox REST API for querying the desired network state.
target_output.security_and_compliance: The infrastructure is highly sensitive, governing critical internet traffic routes. The management plane is strictly air-gapped from the data plane, utilizing dedicated physical OOB management switches. Access to the automation servers requires hardware MFA tokens (e.g., YubiKey) and strict IP allowlisting from designated NOC physical locations. All device configurations are backed up securely, and SSH key rotation is enforced every 30 days via automated scripts to prevent credential harvesting.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: The Python automation scripts are version-controlled in an internal GitLab repository. GitLab CI executes syntax linting and dry-run deployments against virtualized router instances (e.g., Cisco CML or Juniper vMX) before pushing the verified scripts to the production management VMs. Rollbacks are instantaneous; the Python script simply requests the router to load its previously committed configuration file.
target_output.observability: Grafana dashboards provide the NOC with a unified view of the global backbone. Alerts are triggered on physical layer metrics—such as a slow, multi-day decline in optical receive power (dBm)—indicating sea-floor cable degradation, allowing engineers to dispatch repair ships proactively before the fiber snaps completely.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Submarine cable capacity is astronomically expensive. By automating traffic rebalancing during a fault, the organization avoids purchasing excessive, idle backup capacity. The software automation tier scales effortlessly on cheap, localized VMs; it only requires minimal compute to process the gRPC telemetry and execute intermittent NETCONF SSH sessions, keeping operational overhead remarkably low.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (MVP): Configure BFD on all core routers to guarantee the 2-second failover constraint natively. Phase 2: Deploy gRPC telemetry streaming to InfluxDB and build NOC Grafana dashboards. Phase 3: Populate NetBox with the complete global topology. Phase 4: Implement Python NETCONF automation to handle secondary capacity rebalancing and route injection.
target_output.development_guidelines: Never use screen-scraping (CLI text parsing) via Netmiko for configuration management; always use structured data models via NETCONF or RESTCONF to ensure deterministic execution. Ensure all Python automation scripts are strictly idempotent, applying changes only if the router's running configuration diverges from the NetBox intent.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: A bug in the Python rebalancing script injects an erroneous BGP aggregate route, inadvertently creating a global routing loop that takes down the entire network. Mitigation: All automated configuration changes must be sandboxed within the router's candidate configuration, evaluated against automated routing loop detection algorithms on the device, and auto-roll back if a connection to the management server is lost. Tradeoff: Relying heavily on hardware protocols like BFD sacrifices some centralized software control but is the absolute only physical way to meet the sub-2-second constraint required to prevent dropping millions of active internet connections.

metadata.notes: Strong network infrastructure architecture focusing on BGP, BFD, and NetBox.

constraints.value: Must process a batch of 96 patient samples simultaneously within 12 hours

reasoner_reviews.product: The lab runs sequencing machines overnight. The results must be ready for the geneticists' morning review.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Nextflow submitting jobs to Slurm handles the 96-sample batch easily. However, GATK HaplotypeCaller is famously slow. Processing a whole exome sequentially takes too long. We must implement scatter-gather by chunking the genome into smaller intervals (e.g., by chromosome).
reasoner_reviews.security: Data must remain on the on-premise Lustre file system. Do not use cloud storage like S3, per the air-gapped constraint.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Scatter-gather creates thousands of tiny BAM files that must be merged. If this merge runs on a single core, it bottlenecks the 12-hour limit. The merge step must also be heavily parallelized.

backend.orchestration: Nextflow executing `.nf` scripts, managing dependencies via Singularity containers
backend.compute: Slurm array jobs executing BWA-MEM and GATK tasks across hundreds of CPU cores

devops.storage: Lustre File System handling concurrent I/O from 500+ Slurm worker nodes during scatter steps

ISS-21.description: Slurm queue congestion. Launching a scatter-gather workflow for 96 samples generates over 15,000 micro-jobs. This overwhelms the Slurm controller daemon (slurmctld), causing massive scheduling delays and breaching the 12-hour processing constraint.

revision_memory.round_1: Naive scatter-gather implementation generating thousands of tiny jobs per chromosome.
revision_memory.changes_for_round_2: Addressed ISS-21. Optimized the Nextflow scatter logic by grouping genomic intervals into evenly balanced, larger chunks (e.g., 50 chunks per sample instead of 1000). Utilized Nextflow's job grouping (`maxForks` and `submitRateLimit`) to prevent hammering the Slurm controller, easily meeting the 12-hour processing constraint.

previous_audits: Audit 1: HPC administrators reported the pipeline effectively DDoSed the job scheduler, freezing cluster access for other researchers.

previous_plan.title: Exome Pipeline V1
previous_plan.summary: Nextflow script with excessive scattering.

best_plan.title: Exome Pipeline V1
best_plan.summary: Nextflow script with excessive scattering.

target_output.thinking_summary: Revising the Genomic Variant Calling pipeline to address Slurm queue congestion (ISS-21). Optimizing Nextflow's scatter-gather logic to group intervals and rate-limit job submission. Using Singularity, Lustre, and Slurm on an on-premise air-gapped HPC. Quality set to 'strong'.

fix_report.action_taken: Refactored the Nextflow scatter-gather strategy. Instead of scattering by individual genomic intervals (creating 15,000+ jobs), intervals are now dynamically grouped into 50 evenly balanced, multi-megabase chunks per sample. Configured Nextflow with `submitRateLimit` to prevent overwhelming the Slurm daemon.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Drastically reduces Slurm controller congestion. The 96-sample batch processes efficiently within approximately 8 hours, fully complying with the 12-hour clinical turnaround constraint without starving other cluster users.

target_output.title: Clinical Exome Variant Calling Pipeline
target_output.executive_summary: A highly automated, high-performance bioinformatics pipeline designed to execute the GATK Best Practices for whole exome sequencing. Tailored for clinical geneticists diagnosing rare diseases, the tool orchestrates complex data transformations entirely within an air-gapped, on-premise High-Performance Computing (HPC) environment. By leveraging Nextflow to manage distributed Slurm workloads and optimizing scatter-gather execution logic, the architecture guarantees the processing of massive 96-patient sample batches well within the critical 12-hour overnight turnaround window, strictly adhering to HIPAA privacy mandates.
target_output.architecture_overview: The pipeline operates exclusively on a local Linux HPC cluster, ensuring complete data sovereignty. Orchestration is driven by Nextflow, which interprets a declarative Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) of bioinformatics tasks. To resolve severe scheduling bottlenecks (ISS-21), the pipeline optimizes its scatter-gather logic: raw FASTQ reads are aligned using BWA-MEM, and before variant calling, the genomic targets are grouped into balanced chunks rather than highly granular intervals. Nextflow submits these chunked jobs to the Slurm workload manager, rate-limiting the submission to prevent overloading the `slurmctld` daemon. All heavy disk I/O operations—critical for genomic sorting and duplicate marking—execute directly against a high-bandwidth Lustre parallel file system. Dependencies for each step (GATK, Samtools) are strictly encapsulated within Singularity containers to guarantee bit-for-bit scientific reproducibility without requiring root access on the compute nodes.
target_output.technology_stack: Workflow Orchestrator: Nextflow. Workload Manager: Slurm. Bioinformatics Core: BWA-MEM, GATK 4 (Java), Samtools. Scripting: Bash, Groovy. Containerization: Singularity. File System: Lustre Parallel File System. Infrastructure: On-Premise Linux HPC Cluster.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Balanced Scatter-Gather Execution partitioning the exome into optimal computational chunks to maximize CPU utilization without overwhelming the cluster scheduler. Containerized Bioinformatics Toolchain packaging complex Java and C dependencies into immutable Singularity images to ensure clinical validity. High-Throughput Read Alignment utilizing BWA-MEM to accurately map millions of short DNA reads against the hg38 human reference genome. Automated Variant Calling running GATK HaplotypeCaller to identify crucial single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) responsible for rare genetic disorders.
target_output.system_components: Nextflow Head Node executing the master Groovy script and tracking task state. Slurm Controller Daemon (`slurmctld`) allocating physical CPU resources. HPC Compute Nodes running the Singularity instances. Lustre Storage Array delivering massive parallel read/write throughput for intermediate BAM files. Singularity Image Registry caching the immutable bioinformatics tools locally.
target_output.workflows: Overnight Batch Processing: Sequencing machines deposit 96 paired-end FASTQ files onto Lustre. A cron job initiates `nextflow run exome.nf`. Nextflow submits 96 BWA-MEM alignment jobs to Slurm. Slurm allocates 10 cores per job. Nodes write intermediate BAM files back to Lustre. Nextflow triggers the `MarkDuplicates` phase. Nextflow evaluates the target bed file and groups the genomic intervals into 50 chunks per sample. It submits 4,800 GATK HaplotypeCaller jobs to Slurm, restricted to 100 submissions per second. The cluster crunches the variants. Finally, a gather task merges the chunks into a final VCF per patient. The pipeline completes at 6:00 AM, completing the 96-sample batch in 8 hours.
target_output.data_model: Data consists entirely of massive flat files. Inputs: FASTQ (raw sequence reads). Intermediates: BAM/CRAM (binary alignment maps, 10GB+ per exome). Outputs: VCF (Variant Call Format text files). No relational database is utilized in the data plane. Nextflow tracks pipeline execution state and cached hashes in a local hidden `.nextflow` directory using a lightweight LevelDB instance.
target_output.api_design: No network APIs exist. Interactions are exclusively command-line based. Execution relies on Bash shell commands and Slurm directives (`sbatch`, `squeue`). Pipeline parameters are injected via JSON/YAML configuration files: `nextflow run main.nf -params-file batch_config.json`.
target_output.security_and_compliance: The architecture guarantees strict HIPAA compliance through absolute physical and logical air-gapping. The HPC cluster possesses no route to the public internet. Scientific reproducibility, mandated by CLIA/CAP guidelines, is ensured by executing all tools exclusively via pinned Singularity container images, preventing silent updates to the GATK algorithms from altering clinical outcomes. Linux user groups and strict POSIX file permissions restrict access to the raw patient genomic data on the Lustre drives.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: The Nextflow codebase is version-controlled in a local, self-hosted GitLab instance. Operations personnel deploy updates by pulling the latest Git tag onto the HPC head node. Singularity containers (`.sif` files) are built on a separate internet-connected machine, transferred via secure USB or restricted jump host to the cluster, and referenced using absolute file paths in the Nextflow configuration to prevent runtime download failures.
target_output.observability: Observability relies heavily on the native Slurm logging commands and the Nextflow execution report. Upon completion, Nextflow generates a comprehensive HTML timeline detailing CPU utilization, memory consumption, and I/O metrics for every single bioinformatics task, allowing bioinformaticians to pinpoint performance regressions instantly.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Hardware CapEx is a sunk cost for the laboratory. The architectural shift to balanced scatter-gather execution radically optimizes resource usage, allowing the existing HPC hardware to comfortably process 96 samples within the 12-hour limit. If sample volume increases to 200 per night, the pipeline easily scales by purchasing and joining additional bare-metal compute nodes to the Slurm cluster, requiring zero changes to the underlying Nextflow logic.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (MVP): Single-sample sequential execution using BWA-MEM and GATK. Phase 2: Nextflow integration and Singularity containerization. Phase 3: Slurm integration and naive scatter-gather parallelization. Phase 4: Chunk optimization and job rate-limiting to permanently resolve the Slurm controller congestion issues.
target_output.development_guidelines: Never hardcode file paths or thread counts directly in the Bash scripts; all variables must be abstracted into the `nextflow.config` file to allow seamless scaling across different HPC environments. All intermediate BAM files must be aggressively deleted using Nextflow's cleanup directives immediately after variant calling to prevent the Lustre filesystem from reaching 100% capacity mid-batch.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: A sudden hardware failure on a compute node corrupts a running GATK task. Mitigation: Nextflow is configured with `errorStrategy = 'retry'`. It will automatically detect the failure, delete the corrupted partial output file, and resubmit the task to a healthy node in the Slurm queue. Tradeoff: Executing entirely on-premise avoids massive cloud egress fees for terabyte-sized datasets and guarantees strict PHI security, but rigidly caps the laboratory's maximum daily processing throughput based entirely on the physical hardware currently racked in their data center.

metadata.notes: Strong bioinformatics pipeline focusing on Nextflow and Slurm optimization.

project_goal.value: Point of Sale (POS) system for quick-service restaurants

capabilities.rationale: UI, local DB, hardware printing, payment gateway

complexity_level.rationale: Local network routing to kitchen displays

risk_level.rationale: Handles credit card payments

data_sensitivity.rationale: Payment data and daily revenue totals

external_exposure.rationale: Syncs with cloud reporting and payment gateways

feature_scope.value: Order entry, payment processing, kitchen ticket printing, offline mode

mvp_scope.value: Take orders, print receipts, and process card payments via Stripe Terminal

security_baseline.value: PCI P2PE (Point-to-Point Encryption)

constraints.value: UI must respond to touch inputs in under 100ms

execution_preference.rationale: Internet goes down often in restaurants

reasoner_reviews.product: During lunch rush, cashiers tap the screen 5 times a second. Any UI lag ruins their flow.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Vue 3 is great, but we must strictly avoid deep reactivity reactivity tracking on the massive menu catalog to ensure the 100ms constraint. Use `shallowRef` for the menu data.
reasoner_reviews.security: To ensure we are completely out of PCI scope, the POS application must never touch credit card data. The application will trigger the Stripe Terminal reader via local LAN, and Stripe handles the encryption directly.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: For the offline requirement, PouchDB running inside the browser process syncing to a cloud CouchDB handles the replication seamlessly.

frontend.ui: Vue 3 SPA optimized for touch, utilizing standard IPC to communicate with the receipt printer

backend.hardware: Node.js utilizing `escpos` library to send raw byte commands to USB thermal printers

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing an offline-first Restaurant POS using Electron, Vue 3, PouchDB, and CouchDB. Focus on 100ms UI responsiveness constraint and PCI P2PE compliance via Stripe Terminal. First-pass execution, 'moderate' quality profile.
target_output.title: Quick-Service Restaurant POS System
target_output.executive_summary: A highly responsive, offline-first Point of Sale (POS) desktop application designed for fast-paced quick-service restaurants. Built using the Electron framework with a Vue 3 frontend, the system prioritizes extreme touch responsiveness and reliable offline operation to ensure lunch rushes are never interrupted by internet outages. By leveraging PouchDB for seamless background cloud synchronization and strictly enforcing Point-to-Point Encryption (P2PE) via Stripe Terminal, the architecture guarantees robust financial security while completely removing the merchant's hardware from PCI-DSS compliance scope.
target_output.architecture_overview: The POS operates as an Electron desktop application deployed on Windows 10 IoT Enterprise touch-screen kiosks. The architecture is strictly offline-first. The Vue 3 frontend interacts with a local PouchDB instance, allowing cashiers to ring up orders instantly without network latency. When an internet connection is available, PouchDB utilizes its built-in master-master replication protocol to sync transactional data to a centralized cloud-hosted CouchDB cluster for managerial reporting. The Node.js main process handles critical local hardware integrations, utilizing the `escpos` library to communicate via USB with thermal receipt printers. For payment processing, the application never intercepts raw card data. Instead, it issues a localized REST command to a physical Stripe Terminal reader, which encrypts the swipe/dip via P2PE and communicates directly with Stripe's servers, returning only a secure payment token to the POS.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Vue 3, Tailwind CSS, Electron. Backend (Local IPC): Node.js. Database (Local): PouchDB. Database (Cloud): CouchDB. Hardware Integration: `escpos` (Printers), Stripe Terminal SDK. OS: Windows 10 IoT Enterprise. CI/CD: GitHub Actions.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Ultra-Fast Order Entry utilizing shallow reactivity in Vue 3 to ensure menu tapping responds in under 100ms. Offline-First Synchronization allowing continuous cash and offline-cached credit card transactions during ISP outages. PCI-Scopeless Payments offloading all sensitive cardholder data capture directly to the physical Stripe Terminal hardware. Hardware Receipt Printing dispatching raw ESC/POS byte commands directly over USB to standard thermal kitchen and front-of-house printers.
target_output.system_components: Electron Renderer Process executing the Vue 3 interface and PouchDB sync logic. Electron Main Process executing USB printer drivers and system tray logic. Local PouchDB Database holding the day's menu and transactions. Cloud CouchDB Cluster acting as the master aggregator for all franchise locations. Stripe Terminal Reader handling physical card interactions.
target_output.workflows: Order Checkout: Cashier enters their 4-digit PIN. They tap 'Burger' and 'Fries'. Vue 3 updates the UI instantly. Cashier taps 'Pay with Card'. The Vue app sends an IPC message to the Node.js backend to initiate payment for $12.50. Node.js calls the Stripe Terminal SDK. The physical card reader lights up. The customer taps their card. The reader encrypts the data and confirms the charge with Stripe over the internet. The reader signals success to Node.js. Node.js tells Vue. Vue writes the completed `Order` document to the local PouchDB. PouchDB immediately begins replicating this new document to the cloud CouchDB. Node.js sends an ESC/POS byte array to the USB thermal printer, printing the receipt.
target_output.data_model: NoSQL Document Schema (PouchDB/CouchDB). `menu_items`: `{"_id": "item_123", "name": "Burger", "price_cents": 800, "category": "Mains"}`. `orders`: `{"_id": "ord_456", "type": "order", "total_cents": 1250, "items": [...], "payment_status": "paid", "stripe_token": "pi_123", "timestamp": 1678882000}`. Schema validation occurs at the application level prior to insertion.
target_output.api_design: Internal communication utilizes Electron IPC channels (`ipcRenderer.invoke`). External payment communication uses the localized Stripe Terminal REST protocol over the local LAN. Cloud synchronization relies on the CouchDB Replication Protocol over standard HTTPS.
target_output.security_and_compliance: The system effectively neutralizes PCI-DSS compliance burdens by strictly implementing Point-to-Point Encryption (P2PE) via Stripe Terminal; the Windows POS hardware never sees or stores raw Primary Account Numbers (PAN). Employee authentication is optimized for speed using 4-digit PINs, mapped to specific roles (Cashier vs Manager) to authorize discounts or refunds. Local databases are secured via standard Windows IoT full-disk encryption to prevent data tampering if the physical kiosk is stolen.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: The application is compiled using `electron-builder` into a standard Windows executable. Deployments to the restaurant kiosks are managed remotely via a Mobile Device Management (MDM) solution, ensuring all terminals across the franchise run identical software versions. Centralized menu updates are simply pushed to the cloud CouchDB, which automatically replicates down to all active PouchDB clients worldwide.
target_output.observability: Due to the offline requirement, observability relies heavily on local rotating text logs managed by the `winston` logging library in Node.js. These logs capture USB printer disconnects, Stripe Terminal network timeouts, and PouchDB replication conflicts. If the system is online, these metrics are periodically batched and shipped to a centralized monitoring dashboard.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Hardware costs are the primary factor, requiring Windows kiosks and Stripe hardware readers. Software scaling is remarkably elegant; because every terminal hosts its own independent PouchDB database, adding 10 more cash registers to a store introduces zero additional latency or database locking contention. The centralized CouchDB cluster scales horizontally to aggregate the reporting data across thousands of distributed franchises.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (MVP): Vue 3 UI, PouchDB local storage, and manual cash checkout. Phase 2: Implementation of the `escpos` hardware integration for receipt printing. Phase 3: Stripe Terminal integration for secure credit card processing. Phase 4: Cloud CouchDB deployment and background master-master synchronization logic.
target_output.development_guidelines: Frontend developers must utilize `shallowRef` for loading the master menu catalog to prevent Vue's reactivity system from recursively parsing thousands of menu items, which would violate the 100ms touch constraint. All monetary values must be calculated as integers (cents) to avoid floating-point discrepancies in the final receipt totals.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: A 48-hour internet outage causes the local PouchDB database to grow significantly, delaying end-of-day managerial reporting. Mitigation: The system operates normally offline, but UI warnings prompt the manager to reboot the store router to ensure data eventually reaches the cloud ledger. Tradeoff: Adopting a NoSQL PouchDB/CouchDB synchronization model perfectly solves the offline requirement, but makes complex relational queries (like "Top selling items across all stores grouped by region") much harder to write compared to a standard SQL database, requiring secondary data warehouses for business intelligence.

metadata.notes: Moderate quality POS architecture highlighting offline-first PouchDB.

project_goal.value: Command-line tool to analyze code diffs and generate automated Pull Request reviews
project_goal.rationale: Improve code quality and catch security flaws early

project_class.rationale: Executed locally or in CI pipelines

complexity_level.rationale: Git integration and prompt orchestration

risk_level.rationale: Advisory tool only, does not block merges automatically

external_exposure.rationale: Runs strictly on local dev machines or internal CI runners

feature_scope.value: Git diff parsing, code chunking, LLM review generation, markdown output

mvp_scope.value: Review staged Git changes and print a summary of potential bugs and optimizations

security_baseline.value: Exclude sensitive files (e.g., .env, secrets) from being sent to the LLM

backend_stack.rationale: Fast prototyping, excellent tooling via Commander.js

future_scope.value: Automated posting of inline comments to GitHub PRs

constraints.value: Must never send more than 128,000 tokens to the LLM to prevent context limit errors

reasoner_reviews.product: Engineers will ignore the tool if it suggests trivial style changes like tabs vs spaces. It must focus on logic bugs and security flaws.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: TypeScript with Commander.js is perfect. We can use the `simple-git` library to extract the diffs. We need a tokenizer library like `tiktoken` to accurately measure the payload before sending it to OpenAI.
reasoner_reviews.security: Accidentally sending API keys to OpenAI is a huge risk. We must use a library like `gitleaks` or regex filters to aggressively block files ending in `.env`, `.pem`, or containing secret-like strings.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Massive PRs will easily exceed the 128k token limit. We cannot just pass the whole diff. We must implement a chunking strategy, reviewing files individually or in small batches, and then use a final map-reduce prompt to synthesize the overall review.

backend.cli: Commander.js for CLI arguments, Ora for terminal spinners
backend.git: `git diff --staged` parsing to extract only the changed lines

aillm.chunking: Group files into batches < 100k tokens, summarize individually, merge reviews

ISS-12.description: Context Window Exhaustion. The tool crashed during a large repository refactor because it attempted to send a 400,000-token `package-lock.json` and a massive auto-generated GraphQL schema file in a single prompt to GPT-4o.

revision_memory.round_1: Naive implementation sending the entire `git diff` output to OpenAI in one prompt.
revision_memory.changes_for_round_2: Addressed ISS-12. Implemented strict file exclusion rules (ignoring `*-lock.json`, `.svg`, and `generated/` folders). Introduced `tiktoken` to measure payload size. Diffs are now batched into safe 80k-token chunks, processed concurrently, and aggregated, permanently fixing the context window crashes.

previous_audits: Audit 1: Developers abandoned the tool because it crashed on any Pull Request larger than 20 files.

previous_plan.summary: TypeScript CLI with naive monolithic prompting.

best_plan.title: AI Reviewer V1
best_plan.summary: TypeScript CLI with naive monolithic prompting.

target_output.thinking_summary: Revising the AI Code Reviewer CLI to fix context window exhaustion (ISS-12). Implementing file exclusions (lockfiles) and token-based chunking (`tiktoken`) with a map-reduce summarization strategy. Uses Node.js, Commander.js, and OpenAI GPT-4o. Quality set to 'strong'.

fix_report.action_taken: Refactored the Git diff extraction pipeline. Implemented hardcoded exclusions for auto-generated files (e.g., `package-lock.json`, minified bundles). Integrated `tiktoken` to calculate precise token counts. Massive diffs are now chunked into 80,000-token batches, processed concurrently by GPT-4o, and synthesized via a map-reduce summarization step.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Eliminates OpenAI context window errors. The tool now seamlessly handles massive, multi-thousand line Pull Requests without crashing, successfully keeping prompts within the strict 128k limit.

target_output.title: AI-Powered Code Review CLI
target_output.executive_summary: A developer-centric command-line interface (CLI) tool designed to accelerate code reviews and detect logic flaws prior to deployment. Executed locally or within CI/CD pipelines, the tool utilizes Node.js and OpenAI's GPT-4o to analyze Git diffs. To ensure high reliability and resolve critical context-limit crashes, the architecture employs aggressive file filtering and token-aware batching logic. This guarantees that massive repository refactors are processed efficiently without exceeding LLM token constraints, delivering actionable, markdown-formatted code quality insights directly to software engineers.
target_output.architecture_overview: The tool is distributed as a global Node.js package (`npm install -g ai-reviewer`). It utilizes `Commander.js` for argument parsing and terminal interaction. Upon execution, the tool invokes the local Git binary to extract the current staged changes or a specific branch diff. To resolve token exhaustion vulnerabilities (ISS-12), the payload is passed through a strict sanitization layer that automatically strips out lockfiles, binaries, and generated assets. A `tiktoken` encoder counts the remaining tokens. If the payload exceeds 80,000 tokens, the tool splits the diff into logical, file-based chunks. These chunks are sent concurrently to the OpenAI GPT-4o REST API using `Promise.all`. The LLM's system prompt strictly instructs it to ignore stylistic nitpicks and focus on security, race conditions, and algorithmic complexity. A final mapping prompt synthesizes the individual chunk reviews into a cohesive markdown report output to standard out (`stdout`).
target_output.technology_stack: Language: TypeScript, Node.js 20. CLI Framework: Commander.js. Prompting: OpenAI Node.js SDK (GPT-4o). Tokenization: `tiktoken` (jsTiktoken). Git Integration: `simple-git`. Terminal UI: `ora` (Spinners), `chalk` (Colors). Distribution: Internal NPM Registry.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Context-Aware Diff Parsing extracting only the inserted and deleted lines to minimize noise. Token-Optimized Batching partitioning massive code reviews into mathematically safe chunks to prevent API rejections. Aggressive Secret Redaction filtering out `.env` files and utilizing regex to mask potential API keys before cloud transmission. Intelligent Map-Reduce Summarization merging multiple disparate API responses into a unified, deduplicated list of architectural concerns.
target_output.system_components: CLI Entrypoint defining flags (e.g., `--staged`, `--branch main`). Git Diff Extractor executing local shell commands. Payload Sanitizer enforcing exclusion rules. Token Counter calculating the exact BPE (Byte Pair Encoding) length. OpenAI Orchestrator managing asynchronous network requests and retries. Markdown Formatter styling the final terminal output.
target_output.workflows: Review Execution: Developer stages 30 files and types `ai-reviewer scan --staged`. The CLI extracts the diff. The Sanitizer drops `package-lock.json` and a `logo.svg`, reducing the file count to 28. The Token Counter evaluates the diff at 150,000 tokens. The Orchestrator splits this into two chunks (80k and 70k tokens). Two concurrent HTTP POSTs are dispatched to OpenAI. The API returns two JSON arrays of potential bugs. The CLI sends a third, final request to OpenAI containing both arrays, requesting a deduplicated summary. The final markdown is rendered to the terminal in green/red text, highlighting a detected race condition in `auth.ts`.
target_output.data_model: Stateless execution. Intermediate memory models utilize TypeScript interfaces: `interface CodeChunk { fileName: string; diff: string; tokenCount: number; }`. The AI response enforces a strict JSON schema via OpenAI's Function Calling: `{"file": "string", "line": "number", "severity": "high", "description": "string"}`.
target_output.api_design: No internal APIs exposed. Makes outbound REST calls to `https://api.openai.com/v1/chat/completions`. Authentication relies on an `OPENAI_API_KEY` environment variable securely loaded into the developer's local `~/.zshrc` or bash profile.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Sending proprietary corporate source code to a third-party cloud provider poses a significant risk. This is mitigated by exclusively utilizing OpenAI's Enterprise API tier, which legally guarantees that submitted payloads are not retained for training future models. To prevent catastrophic secret leakage, the CLI implements a hardcoded deny-list for sensitive extensions (e.g., `.pem`, `.key`) and executes a regex sweep to redact strings resembling AWS keys or JWTs before the payload ever leaves the developer's local machine.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: The TypeScript code is compiled to raw JavaScript using `esbuild` to eliminate runtime dependencies and minimize installation time. It is published to a private corporate Nexus/NPM registry. Developers update the tool locally via `npm update -g ai-reviewer`. When executed in CI pipelines (e.g., GitHub Actions), the tool uses a dedicated Service Account API key injected via GitHub Secrets.
target_output.observability: Terminal output provides an interactive spinner (`ora`) detailing the current execution phase (e.g., "Analyzing chunk 1/2..."). If the OpenAI API returns a 429 Rate Limit error, the CLI prints a descriptive error to `stderr` and gracefully exits. Extensive debugging logs can be enabled via a `--verbose` flag, printing the exact chunk boundaries and token counts for internal tool maintenance.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: API costs are heavily dependent on GPT-4o usage. By implementing the sanitization layer that strips out lockfiles and generated code (which are useless for AI review), the architecture drastically reduces input token volume, saving significant organizational budget. Scaling is constrained only by OpenAI's account-level rate limits; concurrent processing (`Promise.all`) maximizes throughput for the individual developer.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (MVP): Basic single-prompt integration with GPT-4 for small diffs. Phase 2: Integration of `tiktoken` and the chunking/batching logic to resolve context window crashes. Phase 3: Aggressive secret redaction and file extension exclusion layers. Phase 4: Integration into GitHub Actions to automatically post the generated markdown as a comment on Pull Requests.
target_output.development_guidelines: All API calls to OpenAI must implement the `p-retry` library with exponential backoff to handle transient network issues or rate limits gracefully. The system prompt must be version-controlled independently of the source code, allowing prompt engineers to refine the AI's bug-hunting accuracy without requiring a full NPM package release.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: The LLM confidently hallucinates a non-existent bug, causing engineers to waste time debugging phantom issues. Mitigation: The system prompt explicitly instructs the model to only report issues with a high degree of confidence, and all CLI output is heavily prefixed with disclaimers that it is an advisory tool requiring human verification. Tradeoff: Utilizing a map-reduce chunking strategy to bypass token limits inherently prevents the LLM from seeing the entire codebase simultaneously, slightly increasing the risk that it might miss complex, cross-file architectural bugs.

metadata.notes: Strong CLI architecture focusing on LLM token management and batching.

project_goal.value: Mobile application for patients to view test results and message doctors securely

complexity_level.rationale: UI wrapper around existing hospital APIs

risk_level.rationale: Handles highly sensitive medical records

feature_scope.value: Dashboard, lab result viewer, secure messaging inbox, appointment reminders

mvp_scope.value: View PDF lab results and send text messages to primary care physician

security_baseline.value: No PHI stored on local device, biometric app lock
security_baseline.rationale: Prevent data leakage on lost phones

backend_stack.value: None (Connects directly to Epic FHIR APIs)

constraints.value: App must not cache PDF lab results locally under any circumstances

execution_preference.value: Clean, accessible UI for elderly patients

reasoner_reviews.product: The UI must have large fonts and high contrast. Many of our patients are over 65.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Since this is a stateless client connecting directly to Epic's FHIR APIs, Flutter is perfect. We can use the `url_launcher` or a secure webview to handle the OAuth2 SMART on FHIR login flow.
reasoner_reviews.security: To satisfy the strict constraint against caching PDFs locally, we cannot use standard PDF downloader plugins that write to the device's temporary directory. We must stream the PDF bytes directly into an in-memory viewer widget.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Firebase Crashlytics is fine for crashes, but we must explicitly disable analytics and ensure no patient names or MRNs are accidentally included in the crash dump stack traces.
